items.evidence: Rosenberg's narrative describes a Fort Smith Arkansas private attorney who joined Burke Marshall's Civil Rights Division in 1961-62, worked southwest Georgia voter-registration cases with him (including the Sasser/Terrell County deputy-sheriff case and Americus area), and eventually moved to the Community Relations Service. External scholarly confirmation: the Cambridge Core peer-reviewed article 'The Second, Selective Reconstruction' (Studies in American Political Development) names 'Jerome Heilbron' as the CRD trial attorney assigned full-time to Georgia's rural southwest, first arriving in Baker County in July 1961, reporting to John Doar and communicating with Burke Marshall, and pursuing U.S. v. Rooks et al. against Baker County registrars plus Terrell County cases. The Martin Luther King Jr. Institute corpus also references 'Jerome Heilbron of the Civil Rights Division' filing a friend-of-the-court brief in the August 1962 Albany Georgia injunction case. The original Pass 1 candidate had the surname spelling right (Heilbron, not Heilbrun) but the first name wrong (Jerome, not Gerald). Note: the middle initial 'A.' that was applied in the corrected transcript files at row 61.P4.3 ('Jerome A. Heilbron') is NOT independently corroborated in either external source and should be dropped to avoid hallucinating a middle initial that may not exist; the safe canonical rendering is 'Jerome "Jerry" Heilbron'.
